title: Sorting IV — Linear-Time: Counting, Radix, Bucket

questions.question: Why does counting sort break the Ω(n log n) comparison-sort lower bound?
options: It uses a more efficient divide-and-conquer strategy.
It compares elements in groups rather than pairwise.
It never performs pairwise comparisons — it uses arithmetic to compute positions directly.
The lower bound only applies to unstable sorts; counting sort is stable.
questions.explanation: The Ω(n log n) bound applies to algorithms that determine order exclusively through pairwise comparisons. Counting sort computes output positions using counting arithmetic, bypassing the comparison model entirely.

questions.question: Which property is essential for radix sort to produce correct results across multiple digit passes?
options: Each pass must use a comparison-based sort (e.g., merge sort).
Each pass must sort stably, preserving the relative order established by previous passes.
The number of digits must be at most log₂(n).
All input elements must have exactly the same number of digits.
questions.explanation: LSD radix sort processes digits from least to most significant. Each pass must be stable so that when a later pass encounters equal digits, the ordering from earlier (more significant) passes is preserved.

questions.question: Under what input condition does bucket sort achieve O(n) expected time?
options: When the input array contains no duplicate values.
When the input elements are uniformly distributed over the interval.
When the input is already sorted or nearly sorted.
When the number of buckets equals the maximum element value.
questions.explanation: Bucket sort's O(n) expected time relies on the uniformity assumption: each bucket receives an expected constant number of elements, so sorting each bucket is O(1) on average. Without uniformity, buckets can overflow, degrading performance.

questions.question: You need to sort 10 million 32-bit integers with known range [0, 999]. Which approach is most efficient?
options: Quicksort — O(n log n) average, cache-friendly.
Counting sort — O(n + k) where k = 1000, well within linear time.
Bucket sort — requires uniform distribution which isn't guaranteed.
Merge sort — O(n log n) worst-case, stable.
questions.explanation: With k = 1000 and n = 10,000,000, counting sort runs in O(n + k) ≈ O(n). The small key range makes it dramatically faster than any O(n log n) comparison sort. Radix sort would also work but counting sort alone suffices here.

questions.question: Why can't comparison-based sorting beat O(n log n) in the worst case?
options: Because no algorithm has ever achieved it
Because the decision tree for n elements has at least n! leaves, requiring Omega(n log n) height
Because memory access is the bottleneck
Because it would violate the Church-Turing thesis
questions.explanation: Any comparison sort can be modeled as a binary decision tree. With n! possible permutations, the tree must have at least n! leaves, giving a minimum height of log2(n!) = Theta(n log n). This is an information-theoretic lower bound.
